sources.citation: Torche, F. (2014). Intergenerational mobility and inequality: The Latin American case. Annual Review of Sociology, 40, 619–642.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive review of intergenerational mobility patterns in Latin America, including Mexico, and discusses how perceived versus actual mobility shapes attitudes toward inequality.
sources.key_finding: Latin America exhibits lower intergenerational mobility than developed nations, and perceptions of mobility often diverge from objective measures, influencing support for redistributive policies.

sources.citation: Campos-Vázquez, R. M., & Vélez-Grajales, R. (2014). Crisis económica y movilidad social en México. Estudios Económicos, 29(2), 187–224.
sources.relevance: Directly examines intergenerational economic mobility in Mexico using survey data, measuring whether children surpass their parents' socioeconomic status.
sources.key_finding: Intergenerational mobility in Mexico is limited, particularly at the extremes of the income distribution, with persistent poverty traps and elite reproduction.

sources.citation: Alesina, A., Stantcheva, S., & Teso, E. (2018). Intergenerational mobility and preferences for redistribution. American Economic Review, 108(2), 521–554.
sources.relevance: Examines how perceptions of intergenerational mobility (rather than actual mobility) drive policy preferences, directly relevant to the subjective perception items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Individuals who perceive lower intergenerational mobility are more supportive of redistribution, and these perceptions are shaped by personal narratives and media exposure.

sources.citation: Delajara, M., & Graña, D. (2017). Intergenerational social mobility in Mexico and its regions. Documento de Trabajo CEEY, 06/2017.
sources.relevance: Provides regional analysis of social mobility in Mexico, relevant to understanding how respondents' perceptions of youth opportunities may vary geographically.
sources.key_finding: Southern Mexican states exhibit significantly lower intergenerational mobility than northern states, reflecting deep structural inequalities.

sources.citation: Benabou, R., & Ok, E. A. (2001). Social mobility and the demand for redistribution: The POUM hypothesis. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 116(2), 447–487.
sources.relevance: Theoretical framework explaining why individuals who expect upward mobility for themselves or their children may oppose redistribution, linking mobility perceptions to policy attitudes.
sources.key_finding: The 'prospect of upward mobility' (POUM) hypothesis shows that even relatively poor individuals may oppose redistribution if they believe their children will be better off.

causal_relationships.related_construct: trust in institutions and political culture

sources.citation: Feagin, J. R. (1975). Subordinating the poor: Welfare and American beliefs. Prentice-Hall.
sources.relevance: Foundational work establishing the tripartite framework of poverty attributions: individualistic, structural, and fatalistic, which remains the dominant measurement approach.
sources.key_finding: Americans tend to favor individualistic explanations for poverty, but structural attributions increase among lower-income and minority respondents.

sources.citation: Rojas, M. (2008). Experienced poverty and income poverty in Mexico: A subjective well-being approach. World Development, 36(6), 1078–1093.
sources.relevance: Examines how Mexicans define and experience poverty subjectively, comparing income-based definitions with multidimensional and experiential ones.
sources.key_finding: Subjective definitions of poverty in Mexico diverge substantially from income-based measures, suggesting that lay conceptualizations capture dimensions missed by official statistics.

sources.citation: Lepianka, D., Van Oorschot, W., & Gelissen, J. (2009). Popular explanations of poverty: A critical discussion of empirical research. Journal of Social Policy, 38(3), 421–438.
sources.relevance: Systematic review of cross-national research on poverty attributions, providing methodological guidance on measuring causal beliefs about poverty.
sources.key_finding: Poverty attributions are multidimensional and context-dependent, with welfare regime type and national economic conditions moderating whether structural or individual explanations dominate.

sources.citation: Merolla, D. M., Hunt, M. O., & Serpe, R. T. (2011). Concentrated disadvantage and beliefs about the causes of poverty: A multi-level analysis. Sociological Perspectives, 54(2), 205–227.
sources.relevance: Examines how neighborhood-level poverty concentration shapes individual causal attributions, relevant to understanding Mexican respondents in high-poverty contexts.
sources.key_finding: Living in areas of concentrated disadvantage increases structural attributions for poverty, independent of individual socioeconomic status.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3a ed.). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Mexico's official multidimensional poverty measurement framework, directly relevant to how respondents may conceptualize poverty and extreme poverty in survey items.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's official definition incorporates income, social rights deprivations, and social cohesion, providing a multidimensional benchmark against which lay definitions can be compared.

causal_relationships.related_construct: social policy preferences and support for redistribution
personal socioeconomic status and lived experience of poverty
perceptions of discrimination and social inequality

sources.citation: Díaz-Cayeros, A., Estévez, F., & Magaloni, B. (2016). The political logic of poverty relief: Electoral strategies and social policy in Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how Mexican social programs (Progresa/Oportunidades) are shaped by political incentives and how citizens' preferences for program design interact with electoral dynamics.
sources.key_finding: Conditional cash transfer programs in Mexico gained broad public support partly because their targeting criteria aligned with popular notions of deservingness, but political manipulation remained a concern.

sources.citation: Blofield, M., & Martínez Franzoni, J. (2015). Maternalism, co-responsibility, and social equity: A typology of work–family policies. Social Politics, 22(1), 38–59.
sources.relevance: Discusses normative frameworks underlying social policy design in Latin America, relevant to understanding respondents' preferences for guiding principles of social programs.
sources.key_finding: Latin American social policy preferences are shaped by competing logics of maternalism, universalism, and co-responsibility, with implications for how citizens evaluate program fairness.

sources.citation: Haggard, S., & Kaufman, R. R. (2008). Development, democracy, and welfare states: Latin America, East Asia, and Eastern Europe. Princeton University Press.
sources.relevance: Comparative analysis of welfare state development including Mexico, providing context for understanding public preferences about government spending priorities and funding mechanisms.
sources.key_finding: Democratic transitions in Latin America expanded social spending but public preferences for universalism versus targeting remain contested and shape policy outcomes.

sources.citation: Ardanaz, M., & Scartascini, C. (2013). Inequality and personal income tax compliance. IDB Working Paper No. 390.
sources.relevance: Examines how perceptions of inequality and fiscal fairness shape willingness to fund social programs through taxation, directly relevant to preferred funding mechanisms.
sources.key_finding: In highly unequal Latin American societies, perceived unfairness of the tax system reduces willingness to pay taxes for social programs, even among those who support redistribution in principle.

sources.citation: Canache, D. (2012). Citizens' conceptualizations of democracy: Structural complexity, substantive content, and political significance. Comparative Political Studies, 45(9), 1132–1158.
sources.relevance: Examines how Latin American citizens perceive social stratification and its relationship to democratic quality, relevant to the first- vs. second-class citizen framing.
sources.key_finding: Citizens who perceive themselves as second-class are significantly less satisfied with democracy and less trusting of political institutions.

sources.citation: Solís, P. (2017). Discriminación estructural y desigualdad social: Con casos ilustrativos para jóvenes indígenas, mujeres y personas con discapacidad. CONAPRED/CEPAL.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses structural discrimination in Mexico, documenting how poverty intersects with ethnicity, gender, and disability to produce compounded disadvantage.
sources.key_finding: Discrimination against the poor in Mexico is systemic and intersects with indigenous identity and gender, creating layered barriers to social mobility.

sources.citation: Flores, R., & Telles, E. (2012). Social stratification in Mexico: Disentangling color, ethnicity, and class. American Sociological Review, 77(3), 486–494.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how skin color and ethnicity independently predict social stratification in Mexico, relevant to understanding perceived discrimination among the poor.
sources.key_finding: Skin color has an independent effect on socioeconomic outcomes in Mexico beyond ethnicity and class origin, indicating pervasive colorism that compounds poverty-based discrimination.

sources.citation: ENADIS. (2017). Encuesta Nacional sobre Discriminación 2017: Principales resultados. CONAPRED/INEGI.
sources.relevance: National discrimination survey providing baseline data on perceived discrimination in Mexico, including discrimination based on socioeconomic status.
sources.key_finding: Over 20% of Mexicans reported experiencing discrimination based on their appearance or socioeconomic status, with the poor and indigenous populations reporting the highest rates.

causal_relationships.related_construct: objective socioeconomic status and income inequality
perceptions of public security and justice access

sources.citation: Rojas, M. (2007). The complexity of well-being: A life-satisfaction conception and a domains-of-life approach. In I. Gough & J. A. McGregor (Eds.), Wellbeing in developing countries (pp. 259–280). Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Develops a subjective wellbeing framework applied to Mexico, integrating life satisfaction with domain-specific assessments including economic self-evaluation.
sources.key_finding: In Mexico, economic domain satisfaction is a strong but not dominant predictor of overall life satisfaction, with family and health domains also playing major roles.

sources.citation: Graham, C., & Pettinato, S. (2002). Frustrated achievers: Winners, losers, and subjective well-being in new market economies. Journal of Development Studies, 38(4), 100–140.
sources.relevance: Examines how perceived economic agency and control over future outcomes shape subjective wellbeing in developing and transitional economies, including Latin America.
sources.key_finding: Individuals who perceive low personal agency over economic outcomes report lower wellbeing even when objective conditions improve, highlighting the importance of perceived control.

sources.citation: Lora, E. (Ed.). (2008). Beyond facts: Understanding quality of life. Inter-American Development Bank.
sources.relevance: IDB report on subjective quality of life in Latin America, including Mexico, with chapters on how national economic conditions affect personal welfare perceptions.
sources.key_finding: Latin Americans' subjective economic wellbeing is influenced not only by personal income but also by perceptions of macroeconomic conditions and relative standing.

sources.citation: Bandura, A. (1997). Self-efficacy: The exercise of control. W. H. Freeman.
sources.relevance: Foundational theoretical framework for perceived personal agency, directly relevant to survey items measuring respondents' sense of control over their economic future.
sources.key_finding: Self-efficacy beliefs powerfully predict behavioral outcomes and psychological wellbeing, with individuals who perceive greater personal agency showing more resilience and goal-directed behavior.

sources.citation: Veenhoven, R. (2012). Cross-national differences in happiness: Cultural measurement bias or effect of culture? International Journal of Wellbeing, 2(4), 333–353.
sources.relevance: Addresses measurement challenges in cross-cultural subjective wellbeing research, relevant to interpreting Mexican respondents' life satisfaction and agency reports.
sources.key_finding: Cultural factors influence both the level and meaning of subjective wellbeing reports, but measurement bias does not fully account for cross-national differences.

causal_relationships.related_construct: objective economic conditions and employment status
health status and access to healthcare
